agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial commitment by a customer to purchase goods or services, tracked through a lifecycle of states."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing fulfillment and (presumably) releasing any reserved inventory or commitments."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer; represents a point-of-no-return in fulfillment."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Per finance, invoicing is irreversible — once created, the invoice cannot be undone."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created/confirmed in the system. Per operations, this is also the moment invoicing occurs."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy (e.g., restrict cancellation to within 1 hour of placement).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, cancellation should succeed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance states this as a hard financial constraint — invoicing creates an irreversible financial record. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders would corrupt financial integrity (revenue recognition, tax records, audit trail).","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has an associated invoice, any cancellation attempt must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy to lock in pricing. It could be changed (e.g., invoice at shipment, or invoice at pick-pack). However, it is the current stated process.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice must be created immediately as part of the placement process."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says allow it (not yet shipped). BR-2 + BR-3 say reject it (already invoiced at placement). Behavior is undefined until contradiction is resolved."},{"scenario":"Order placed but shipment delayed for days","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: PM expects cancellation is available during this entire window. Finance/Ops rules make it impossible since invoice was created at placement."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"Unspecified. If part of the order ships, can the unshipped portion be cancelled? Also blocked by the invoice contradiction regardless."},{"scenario":"System failure prevents invoice creation at placement","expected_behavior":"If invoicing fails at placement, the order would technically be uninvoiced and cancellable under BR-2, but BR-3 requires the invoice to exist. This is an error-state edge case that needs process definition."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If we implement BR-1 and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, we break financial integrity (phantom invoices with no backing order, revenue recognition errors, tax audit failures). If we implement BR-2+BR-3 strictly, customers can never cancel, leading to chargebacks, refund requests, and customer attrition.","operational":"The resolution of this contradiction determines the entire order lifecycle state machine. Picking the wrong side will require rework across order management, invoicing, fulfillment, and customer service systems.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment — this is an industry norm. A system that invoices at placement and then blocks all cancellation will generate heavy customer service load, negative reviews, and potential regulatory issues (consumer protection laws in many jurisdictions grant cancellation rights).","risk":"Implementing without resolving this contradiction guarantees one stakeholder's constraint is violated. Shipping a broken invariant in finance (corrupted invoices) is far more dangerous than a restrictive cancellation policy, but both paths have serious consequences.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all under current Finance+Ops rules, despite PM intent. Will drive support tickets, chargebacks, and churn."},{"group":"Finance/Accounting","impact":"If cancellation is allowed on invoiced orders, invoice integrity is broken — audit, tax, and revenue recognition all affected."},{"group":"Customer Service","impact":"Will bear the brunt of whichever decision is made — either handling cancellation-denied complaints or processing manual invoice corrections."},{"group":"Operations/Warehouse","impact":"Need clarity on whether to proceed with fulfillment on orders customers want cancelled."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Consumer protection laws (e.g., EU Distance Selling Regulations, US FTC Cooling-Off Rule) may legally require a cancellation window regardless of internal invoicing policy."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The three requirements contain a direct logical contradiction that makes it impossible to define correct behavior. BR-1 (cancel before ship) is incompatible with BR-2 (no cancel after invoice) + BR-3 (invoice at placement). Taken together: orders are invoiced the moment they are placed, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, therefore orders can never be cancelled — directly contradicting the PM requirement. Confidence is below 50 because no acceptance criteria can be safely defined until stakeholders resolve this.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — this is not a matter of interpretation","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is almost certainly a true invariant rooted in financial/legal requirements","The domain language and lifecycle concepts are well-understood"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's requirement should yield","Whether 'invoiced' in Finance's constraint means the same thing Operations intends (e.g., could a 'pro-forma' or 'provisional' invoice satisfy Ops without triggering Finance's constraint?)","Whether legal/regulatory requirements exist that would override all three stakeholders"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholders are describing the same system and order lifecycle","'Invoice' means the same thing to Finance and Operations","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-voiding mechanism that Finance omitted from their constraint"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility. (1) PM says cancel anytime before shipment. (2) Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. (3) Ops says orders are invoiced at placement. Therefore: orders are invoiced immediately → can never be cancelled → PM requirement is impossible. These three statements cannot all be true simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Change invoicing timing — invoice at shipment instead of placement, satisfying PM and Finance but requiring Ops to find another mechanism to lock pricing (e.g., price snapshots without invoicing).","B: Introduce credit notes — keep invoice-at-placement but allow 'cancellation' via a compensating credit note, preserving invoice immutability while enabling functional cancellation. Finance constraint is honored (invoice is never deleted), PM intent is satisfied (customer can effectively cancel).","C: Restrict cancellation window — keep invoice-at-placement and accept that orders cannot be cancelled, overriding the PM requirement. Add a pre-placement 'cart confirmation' step as the last cancellation opportunity.","D: Introduce provisional vs. final invoicing — Ops creates a provisional/pro-forma invoice at placement (locks pricing) that is not subject to Finance's irreversibility constraint. Final invoice is created at shipment. Cancellation is allowed before final invoicing."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) or Option D (provisional invoicing) are most likely to satisfy all parties. Option B is the industry-standard pattern — invoices are never deleted, but credit notes reverse them. This preserves financial integrity while enabling cancellation. However, this requires Finance to confirm that credit notes are an acceptable mechanism. Escalating to all three stakeholders for resolution before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria — the requirements as stated are mutually exclusive.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is impossible to satisfy when BR-2 and BR-3 are both enforced. Implementing any subset without resolution will violate at least one stakeholder's stated constraint."}]}
